ESA & Vibration Monitoring

Vibration monitoring has blind spots. ESA closes them.

Vibration is excellent for accessible rotating equipment. But 30-40% of a typical fleet sits beyond sensor reach: submerged pumps, sealed drives, hazardous zones. ESA monitors those assets from the motor control cabinet. Many customers run both. The question isn't which is better. It's which assets need which method.

Request a Demo See Detection Matrix
Comparison ESA Strengths Vibration Strengths Detection Matrix Better Together Evidence FAQ

Side by side

What each method covers and where it stops

The difference isn't which is better. It's where the sensor sits, what it can reach, and what it costs to deploy.

Criteria ESA (SAM4) Vibration monitoring
Sensor location Motor control cabinet (MCC) On or near the asset
Physical access to asset Not required Required
Installation time <30 minutes per machine Hours to days (varies by access)
Production shutdown None Often required for mounting
Monitoring mode Continuous (24/7) Continuous or periodic (route-based)
Submerged assets Monitored from MCC Cannot mount sensor
ATEX / hazardous zones No ATEX cert needed Requires ATEX-rated equipment
Enclosed / sealed assets No disassembly needed Requires access to housing
Bearing fault detection Detected High resolution
Misalignment / imbalance Detected High resolution
Electrical faults Primary strength Not detectable
Process deviations Cavitation, clogging, efficiency Limited (indirect indicators)
Mechanical fault resolution Good. AI-enhanced detection Excellent on accessible assets
Cost per monitoring point Lower (MCC install) Higher for hard-to-reach assets
Fleet scalability Rapid deployment (1,000+ assets) Slower. Per-asset installation

How to read this table. Each row represents a deployment or operational factor. Checkmarks and crosses indicate capability or constraint. Partial marks show cases where the method applies but with caveats. ESA's defining advantage is reach. Vibration's strength is mechanical resolution on accessible assets.

Beyond vibration's reach

The assets vibration can't monitor

ESA's defining advantage is reach. These scenarios add coverage that vibration physically cannot provide.

Submerged or enclosed

Submersible pumps, sealed compressors, enclosed blowers. ESA monitors from the control cabinet. No disassembly. No access to housing.

Hazardous zones

ATEX areas, confined spaces, flammable atmospheres. ESA sensors stay in the MCC. No explosion risk. No ATEX certification required.

Remote sites

Off-grid pumping stations, remote production lines, distributed assets. ESA transmits via cellular. No site visits for data collection.

Electrical faults

Stator winding degradation, rotor bar defects, insulation breakdown. ESA detects electrical faults. Vibration cannot.

Between vibration rounds

ESA provides continuous 24/7 monitoring on accessible assets, catching faults between scheduled vibration routes.

Fleet-scale speed

Deploy across 1,000+ assets in weeks. MCC installation takes <30 minutes per asset. Vibration deployment takes weeks per asset.

Vibration's strengths

Where vibration has the edge

Vibration monitoring has decades of field validation. For certain faults on accessible assets, it provides resolution ESA doesn't match.

High-resolution mechanical diagnostics

Vibration sensors mounted on machinery provide direct mechanical data. Bearing defect frequencies, gear mesh patterns, resonance analysis. For accessible assets on defined routes, vibration delivers finer spectral detail.

Established practice and standards

Decades of ISO 10816, ISO 20816 validation. Industry standards. Widespread expertise. If your fleet is 60-70% accessible, mature vibration is proven and familiar.

Caveat. These advantages apply to accessible assets. Once a motor sits beyond physical reach, you need ESA. Vibration alone can't solve the coverage problem.

ESA constraints. Detection depends on drive topology (AC induction motors), sampling rate, and load variation. Very low-frequency drives (<5 Hz VFD output) limit spectral resolution. Extremely short duty cycles (<10 seconds) reduce analysis depth.

The 5% we miss. 95% detection rate across 1,135 field faults in the SAM4 fleet. 58 misses. Most were indirect (late-stage mechanical effects on inaccessible assets, extreme load variation masking signals, or drive configurations outside design parameters).

Why this transparency. No monitoring technology covers everything. Stating boundaries builds trust.

Fault-by-fault breakdown

What each method detects

This is a coverage map, not a scorecard. Use both where they add value.

Fault class ESA (SAM4) Vibration Primary method
Bearing damage StrongDetected via current signature modulation StrongHigh spectral resolution on accessible assets Both. Complementary
Misalignment / imbalance StrongLoad torque oscillation visible in current StrongClassical vibration signature. Well-documented Vibration (when accessible)
Stator winding degradation StrongPrimary ESA strength. Inter-turn shorts, insulation breakdown Not detectableElectrical fault. No mechanical vibration signature ESA only
Rotor bar defects StrongSideband analysis around supply frequency LimitedIndirect. Only late-stage mechanical effects ESA primary
Supply asymmetry / power quality StrongVoltage and current imbalance measured directly Not detectableElectrical condition. Outside vibration scope ESA only
Cavitation StrongLoad pattern changes visible in current draw ModerateHigh-frequency noise on accessible pumps ESA primary
Clogging / blockage StrongPower draw deviation from baseline LimitedIndirect flow effects only ESA primary
Efficiency loss / BEP drift StrongPower consumption vs expected operating point Not detectableProcess condition. No vibration signature ESA only
Belt / coupling wear ModerateTorque variation detectable in advanced cases StrongDistinctive frequency patterns on accessible drives Vibration primary
Loose foundation / structural LimitedOnly if load profile is significantly affected StrongSub-harmonic and directional vibration patterns Vibration primary
Gear mesh faults LimitedOnly if gearbox load couples to motor current StrongGear mesh frequency and sidebands. Well-characterised Vibration primary
Strong High detection confidence Moderate Detectable with caveats Limited Indirect or late-stage only Not detectable Outside method's scope

How to read this matrix. Strong means high detection confidence. Moderate means detectable but with conditions or caveats. Limited means indirect or only late-stage. Not detectable means the method is not designed to address this fault class. Primary method column shows which approach is most effective for each fault.

Better together

100% of your fleet. Not 60-70%.

Vibration

60-70%

Accessible assets covered by traditional monitoring

High-resolution mechanical diagnostics. Decades of proven standards. Strong on accessible rotating equipment.

ESA

30-40%

Previously invisible assets now monitored

Submerged, enclosed, hazardous, remote. Electrical faults. Continuous monitoring. From the MCC cabinet.

Combining signal types. Vibration and ESA detect faults through different physics. Vibration reads mechanical motion. ESA reads electrical current signature. When both methods cover the same asset, you get two independent signals. Faults detected by both increase confidence. Faults detected by one only expand your detection map. The overlap isn't duplication. It's validation.

Vibration monitors what sensors can reach.
ESA monitors what sensors cannot.

Together they cover the entire plant.

ESA caught what vibration missed

Schiphol Airport

Bearing damage missed by vibration, caught by ESA

Vibration sensors on the baggage handling gearbox motor showed no alarm. SAM4 detected the developing bearing degradation through current signature analysis. Post-maintenance inspection confirmed the damage. Across the deployment, SAM4 caught all 9 confirmed faults. Vibration missed 100% of them.

9/9 faults detected

DuPont

Faults found where vibration programme was mature

DuPont had a mature vibration programme across critical rotating equipment. ESA found faults vibration had not flagged. Over three years, those detections prevented unplanned shutdowns worth an estimated 1.7 million euro. The vibration programme was working. ESA caught what it could not.

€1.7M estimated costs prevented

"You don't know what you don't know. If you're not looking continuously, you're exposed."

John McCrystal, DuPont

Explore all customer results →

Common questions from reliability teams

Does SAM4 replace my vibration sensors?

No. SAM4 extends your monitoring programme to assets that vibration sensors can't reach. Submerged pumps, sealed blowers, ATEX zones, and remote sites. Where vibration is already installed and delivering value, keep it. SAM4 covers the 30-40% of your fleet that currently has zero visibility.

Many customers run both side by side. Vibration gives depth on accessible assets. ESA gives breadth across the fleet. Together they close more of the monitoring gap than either method alone.

Can ESA detect everything vibration detects?

Not everything. For high-resolution mechanical diagnostics on accessible assets. Specific bearing defect frequencies, gear mesh patterns, resonance analysis. Vibration sensors mounted on the machine provide finer spectral detail. ESA detects the same fault categories (bearings, misalignment, imbalance) but through electrical signatures rather than mechanical vibration. Where ESA adds capability is electrical faults (stator winding, rotor bar, insulation) and process deviations (cavitation, clogging, efficiency loss) that vibration doesn't see.

What if I already have a mature vibration programme?

Good. That means 60-70% of your fleet is covered. SAM4 targets the remaining 30-40% your vibration programme physically can't reach. It also adds continuous monitoring between vibration routes on accessible assets, catching faults that develop between scheduled rounds. Your vibration analysts keep doing what they do best. SAM4 fills the gap in their coverage map. And gives them a second signal type to cross-reference when both methods cover the same asset.

How does SAM4 handle false positives?

Every alert is reviewed by a Samotics reliability engineer before it reaches your team. The AI flags anomalies. A human validates the fault type, assesses severity, and writes a specific recommended action. The result: a 1.8% false positive rate over the past 12 months with zero false anomalies. The 1.8% includes cases where the detection was correct but the fault classification was adjusted after customer feedback. Your maintenance team gets expert-validated insights, not raw alerts to triage. This is a core part of the service model. Not an optional add-on.

How we measure detection performance

True Positive (TP) A SAM4 finding that is confirmed by customer action, independent inspection, or observed failure.
False Positive (FP) A SAM4 finding delivered to the customer that is not confirmed as a real fault.
False Negative (FN) An asset failure that occurred without a prior SAM4 alert during the monitoring period.
False Positive Rate FP / (TP + FP). The proportion of delivered findings that were not confirmed.
Current rate 1.8% over the past 12 months (February 2025. January 2026, n=1,097 delivered findings). Includes false communications. Zero false anomalies in this period.
Scope All validated findings delivered to customers across the active SAM4 fleet. Every alert is reviewed by a Samotics diagnostics engineer before delivery.

We publish this metric because we believe condition monitoring vendors should define their terms. If a vendor quotes an accuracy rate without stating the formula, sample, and time window. Ask them to.
